revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:伊甘神社]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ikan Shrine |image = File:伊甘神社.jpg |location = Shimane Prefecture, Hamada City, Shimofu Town 935, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|34.889|N|132.069|E|region:JP-32|display=inline,title}} |deity = Amatara Hikokunioshihito no Mikoto |status = {{ill|Shikinaisha|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Minor Shrine), Former {{ill|Village Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}} |establishment = May 5, Jōgan 3 (861) |location = {{ill|Hamada|qid=Q432055|1=Hamada, Shimane}} }}
'''Ikan Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|伊甘神社}}, ''Ikan Jinja'') is a Shinto shrine located in Shimofu Town, Hamada City, Shimane Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is believed to be situated on the site of the ancient Iwami Provincial Government Office. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''Engishiki'' and was designated as a village shrine under the old Ranking system. Ikan Shrine also enshrines the {{ill|Sōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōja|qq=}} of Iwami Province, Fuchū Shrine.
== Enshrined Deities == * '''Amatara Hikokunioshihito no Mikoto''': The legendary forebear of the Ono clan, responsible for ancient development in the Iwami region. * '''Mizokui Hime no Mikoto''' (alternative): Referred to in some sources, including the ''Iwami Yaoyoroi'' and ''Naka District History,'' as a deity associated with textile arts and weaving.
== Historical Background == The shrine was founded on May 5, 861, during the Jōgan era. It is mentioned in the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'' (''Chronicles of the Three Reigns'') in 769, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. The name "Ikan" is interpreted in some sources to mean "sweet water springing forth," signifying the origin of the Ikan area. Another theory attributes the name to the "Inokambe," a group credited with early development of the region.
In the modern era, the shrine was designated a village shrine in 1872 and was incorporated into the Association of Shinto Shrines (Association of Shinto Shrines) in 1946.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Reports on Shikinaisha, Vol. 21, San'indo 4.'' Kōgakkan University Press, 1983. * Kenichi Tanikawa, ed. ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Vol. 7: San'in.'' Hakusuisha, 1985. * Iwami Regional Historical Materials Publishing Society, ed. ''Iwami Yaoyoroi.'' Iwami Regional Historical Materials Publishing Society, 1999. * Ikutarō Ōshima, ''Naka District History.'' 1970.
== See Also == * {{ill|Iwami Province|en|Iwami Province|qid=Q1053709|lt=Iwami Province|1=Iwami Province}} * [[Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]]
== External Links == * [http://www.genbu.net/data/iwami/ikan_title.htm Genbutsu no Kioku - Ikan Shrine] (Japanese)
{{Sōja shrines}}
